name: Cambridge Analytica OCEAN Model (cautionary case)
core_claim: Cambridge Analytica (CA) did not invent a new construct; it commercially repackaged the Big Five / OCEAN model (Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism) combined with the Cambridge Psychometrics Centre line of research (Kosinski, Stillwell, Graepel). The core academic claim — distinct from CA's marketing — is twofold. (1) MEASUREMENT: latent psychological traits can be predicted from purely behavioural digital traces (Facebook Likes) without any self-report. Kosinski, Stillwell & Graepel (2013, PNAS) showed Likes alone predict Openness, Extraversion, etc., plus sensitive attributes (sexual orientation, ethnicity, political/religious views, substance use). Youyou, Kosinski & Stillwell (2015, PNAS) showed a Like-based model matched or beat human judges. (2) PERSUASION: Matz, Kosinski, Nave & Stillwell (2017, PNAS) claimed that matching ad CONTENT to inferred personality (e.g. extraverted vs introverted framing of the same product) raises clicks and conversions. The theoretical link to media preference is the same trait-congruence logic that underlies Rentfrow & Gosling's music/film work: people enjoy content whose stylistic and thematic texture matches their dispositions (high Openness → complex, ambiguous, novel content; low Openness → familiar, conventional, resolved content). CA's actual contribution to media-preference science is essentially zero — it is a CAUTIONARY case about (a) over-claiming predictive power, (b) covert inference without consent, and (c) the gap between 'traits are inferrable' and 'inference changes behaviour'.
predictive_validity_for_media: The CA case must be split into the validated academic core and the inflated commercial claims. VALIDATED (measurement): Kosinski et al. 2013 (n≈58,000) reported Like-based prediction of Openness at r≈0.43, Extraversion r≈0.40, and dichotomous sensitive traits at high AUC (e.g. sexual orientation AUC≈0.88 for men, ethnicity≈0.95). Youyou et al. 2015 (n≈86,000) found a model using ~100 Likes correlated with self-reported Big Five better (avg r≈0.56) than human friends (r≈0.49) and approached spouse accuracy. CONTESTED (persuasion): Matz et al. 2017 ran 3 field experiments reaching ~3.5M people; the headline result was that personality-congruent ads produced up to ~40% more clicks and ~50% more conversions than incongruent ads in matched conditions. INFLATED / REFUTED: CA's public claims of decisive electoral persuasion are unsupported — NBC, academic reviewers, and operatives from both US parties called the effectiveness 'very much overstated'; there is essentially NO peer-reviewed evidence CA's psychographic microtargeting moved votes. The Matz study itself was challenged: Sharp et al. 2018 noted the effect held in only 2 of the experiments and argued superior creative (not targeting) drove the difference; Eckles et al. 2018 argued Facebook's own ad-optimization algorithm, not psychology, could explain the gap. The authors rebutted both. For MEDIA preference specifically (the relevant transfer), CA produced nothing; the usable evidence is the adjacent Big-Five-genre literature: Openness is the most robust predictor of preferring complex/unconventional film, TV and music (typical r≈0.2–0.4); Conscientiousness and Openness predict film-genre choice; other traits show weak/inconsistent links. Bottom line: traits are reliably INFERRABLE and modestly predict GENRE and THEMATIC preference; the strong PERSUASION/manipulation claims that made CA famous are unproven.
instrument_design: Two very different 'instruments' coexist in this case. (1) The self-report core is the standard Big Five questionnaire (myPersonality used IPIP-derived items; full IPIP-NEO is 300 items, common forms are the 100-item IPIP, 44-item BFI, 60-item NEO-FFI). These are ordinary Likert (1–5 agree/disagree) instruments, 5–20 minutes. (2) CA's actual operational 'instrument' was NOT a questionnaire administered to targets — it was a BEHAVIOURAL inference model: Facebook Likes (and other traces) fed into a regression/SVD model trained against myPersonality self-reports, producing OCEAN scores with no questions asked of the target. That is the methodological signature of the whole affair: the measurement was covert and trace-based. For a low-friction onboarding context the relevant short forms are the standard ones (NOT a CA artefact): TIPI (Ten-Item Personality Inventory, 2 items/trait, ~1 min, but noisy), BFI-10 (10 items, ~2 min), or single-item Mini-IPIP-style scales. A viable <2-minute, 6–10-item version would prioritise Openness (highest media-preference signal) with 2 items, plus 1–2 items each for Extraversion, Neuroticism, Conscientiousness, Agreeableness — accepting reduced reliability. The CA lesson for instrument design is the opposite of what HelpME2C should copy: behavioural inference without consent is exactly the pattern to avoid.
cold_start_applicability: Mixed and important to disambiguate. The Big Five core IS genuinely useful at cold start: a short self-report quiz yields a stable trait profile before any viewing history exists, and Openness in particular gives real (if modest) signal for cross-medium preference inference (predicting that an Openness-high user will tolerate slow-burn, nonlinear, ambiguous TV/anime even before a single interaction). This is the legitimately transferable part. The CA-SPECIFIC method — inferring OCEAN from behavioural traces — is INAPPLICABLE and undesirable at cold start: it requires exactly the third-party behavioural data (Likes, browsing) that a cold-start, consent-respecting recommender does not and should not have. So the takeaway is: borrow the construct (Big Five via a short consensual quiz), reject the elicitation method (covert trace inference). Signal quality from a 6–10 item self-report quiz is moderate — enough to bias initial ranking and avoid worst mismatches, not enough to be the sole driver. It works for cross-medium inference precisely because personality is medium-agnostic, which is the property HelpME2C needs.
group_compatibility_use: There is no CA-specific two-person model — CA targeted individuals. The two-person application must be built from the underlying Big Five literature, which does have relevant findings. (1) Similarity vs complementarity: for SHARED-activity satisfaction (the joint-watch case), trait SIMILARITY on Openness tends to predict agreement on what counts as enjoyable content, because Openness most directly governs tolerance for unconventional/ambiguous material — a high-Openness + low-Openness pair will systematically clash on slow-burn or nonlinear shows. (2) Aggregation strategies from group-recommender research apply: AVERAGING trait scores is naive and can recommend content neither person likes when traits diverge; LEAST-MISERY (cap by the more constrained partner — e.g. the lower-Openness member sets the ceiling on narrative difficulty, the higher-Neuroticism member sets the floor on tonal darkness/horror) is better supported for avoiding a veto-able bad experience; INTERSECTION works for binary tolerances (both must tolerate violence/horror). Evidence for which strategy predicts JOINT satisfaction in personality terms specifically is thin — most group-rec validation is on rating data, not personality — so this is a design hypothesis, not a settled result. Concrete HelpME2C use: treat divergent Openness as a flag to steer toward middle-difficulty content, and use least-misery on aversive dimensions (horror intensity, darkness) governed by Neuroticism/low-sensation-seeking. [uncertain] Direct empirical evidence that a specific personality-aggregation rule predicts two-person joint viewing satisfaction is weak; this synthesis extrapolates from general group-recommender and Big-Five-dyad literature.
quiz_ux_implications: Direct Likert elicitation of Big Five is workable but carries known biases that matter for an onboarding quiz. (1) Social desirability: Conscientiousness and Agreeableness items are most inflated ('I am organised', 'I am considerate') — for media preference these are low-value traits anyway, so the bias is tolerable. (2) Acquiescence bias (yea-saying) is real on short agree/disagree scales; mitigate with balanced keying (mix positively and negatively worded items) — hard in a 6–10 item set, so accept some noise. (3) Faking is largely irrelevant in a no-stakes recommendation context (unlike hiring), since the user benefits from honest answers — this is a genuine advantage over the CA setting. (4) The strongest UX move is to elicit the HIGH-SIGNAL trait (Openness) more richly and indirectly: rather than 'I am open to new experiences' (abstract, prone to halo answers), use concrete preference forced-choices ('a film that explains everything by the end' vs 'a film that leaves things unresolved') which simultaneously measure Openness AND map directly to theme vocabulary. This gamified/preference-pair format reduces friction and abstraction, and produces immediately actionable signal. The CA cautionary lesson here is about TRANSPARENCY: the quiz must be visibly a quiz (the user knows they are answering personality-adjacent questions), never a covert inference — the opposite of CA's covert Like-scraping.
cross_cultural_validity: The Big Five has the broadest cross-cultural validation of any personality model (McCrae & Costa NEO translations across 50+ cultures; the lexical Big Five replicates reasonably in many languages), which is a point in its favour for HelpME2C's multilingual catalog. BUT there are real breakdowns: (a) the five-factor structure replicates less cleanly in some non-Western, non-literate, or collectivist samples — Agreeableness/Conscientiousness boundaries blur and a distinct sixth factor (Honesty-Humility, the HEXACO addition) appears in some languages; (b) translation of Openness items is notoriously unstable — 'openness to ideas/aesthetics' carries Western-educated connotations that do not translate cleanly, and Openness is exactly the trait HelpME2C most relies on, so this is the sharpest risk; (c) response-style differences (extreme vs midpoint responding) vary systematically by culture and contaminate Likert scores. The CA evidence base (myPersonality) was overwhelmingly Western, English-speaking, Facebook-using — its predictive models do NOT demonstrate cross-cultural validity at all. Practical implication: a Big Five quiz is more transportable than most alternatives, but the Openness items in particular need careful localization (prefer concrete behavioural/preference framings over abstract trait adjectives) and should not be assumed equivalent across the Korean/Japanese/Arabic/Hindi audiences.
privacy_consent_implications: This is the heart of the cautionary case. CA's defining failure was processing inferred personality data WITHOUT valid consent (data harvested via a third-party app under false 'academic' pretences). Under GDPR, personality profiling is high-risk for two reasons: (1) Article 9 special-category data — personality inference can reveal or be a proxy for political opinions, religious/philosophical beliefs, health (Neuroticism↔mental health), and sexual orientation; the Kosinski 2013 paper explicitly showed Likes predict exactly these protected attributes. If processing infers special-category data, Article 9 applies and requires EXPLICIT consent (Art 9(2)(a)) on top of an Article 6 lawful basis — the two are cumulative. (2) Profiling + automated decision-making provisions (Art 22) and transparency duties attach even to non-special trait data. For HelpME2C: collecting Big Five via a transparent, opt-in onboarding quiz used solely for recommendations is defensible IF (a) consent is explicit and specific, (b) the purpose is narrow (recommendation, not profiling for ads/resale), (c) data minimisation is honoured (store derived preference signal, ideally not a raw psychometric profile, or store transiently), and (d) no sensitive inference is made or stored. Per the repo's ADR-0012 and §2 invariants, this maps to: explicit consent, real deletion, no sensitive inference. The safest posture is to treat quiz answers as transient input to a derived preference vector and avoid persisting a labelled OCEAN profile at all — that sidesteps most Article 9 exposure. [uncertain] Whether a transparently collected, recommendation-only Big Five profile is itself 'special category' is jurisdiction- and use-dependent; legal review advised before persisting raw trait scores.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Mapping is strongest for Openness and Neuroticism; weaker for the others. HIGH OPENNESS → nonlinear-storytelling, earned-ambiguity, slow-burn, meditation-on-time, meditation-on-death, magical-realism, character-study, existential-horror, cosmic-horror, satire-of-power, absurdist-comedy (preference for complexity, unresolved endings, formal experimentation, intellectual/aesthetic challenge). LOW OPENNESS → whodunit, heist-and-caper, workplace-procedural, underdog-rise, single-incident-fallout (preference for conventional structure, clear resolution, familiar form). HIGH NEUROTICISM → ambivalent: draws toward grief-and-loss, loneliness-and-isolation, trauma-survival, mental-illness-portrayal (resonance with emotional content) but AVERSE to existential-horror, cosmic-horror, survival-and-endurance at high intensity (anxiety regulation) — this is the key 'floor' trait for a partner aversion model. LOW NEUROTICISM / HIGH sensation-seeking (an Openness/Extraversion-adjacent facet) → chase-and-escape, survival-and-endurance, post-apocalyptic-survival, vigilante-justice, war-and-its-toll (arousal, danger, high stakes). HIGH EXTRAVERSION → ensemble-mosaic, found-family, friendship-tested, celebrity-and-fame, dark-comedy (social, energetic, people-dense narratives); LOW EXTRAVERSION → character-study, loneliness-and-isolation, slow-burn (interiority). HIGH AGREEABLENESS → redemption-arc, found-family, betrayal-and-forgiveness, platonic-soulmates, coming-of-age (prosocial warmth); LOW AGREEABLENESS → power-corrupts, moral-compromise, vengeance-and-its-cost, rise-and-fall, satire-of-power, complicity-with-evil (tolerance for antiheroes and moral darkness). HIGH CONSCIENTIOUSNESS → mystery-investigation, workplace-procedural, biographical-portrait (order, competence, structure). These mappings are directional hypotheses grounded in the Openness-genre literature, NOT measured against this specific vocabulary. [uncertain] The specific Big-Five → theme-slug links beyond Openness are inferred design hypotheses, not empirically validated against HelpME2C's vocabulary.
implementation_recommendation: PARTIAL — include the construct, hard-reject the method. (1) INCLUDE a short, transparent, opt-in Big Five quiz at onboarding, weighted heavily toward Openness (the only trait with robust media-preference validity): ~6–8 items total — 2 Openness, 1–2 Neuroticism (for the aversion/'floor' model used in two-person least-misery), 1 each Extraversion/Agreeableness/Conscientiousness. Target <2 minutes. (2) PREFER concrete preference-pair / forced-choice framings over abstract trait adjectives (reduces social-desirability, acquiescence, and cross-cultural translation problems, and maps directly to theme vocabulary). (3) USE the output as a soft prior on theme-vocabulary weights and a cold-start ranking bias, NOT as a hard filter and NOT as a persisted labelled OCEAN profile — store a derived preference vector to minimise Article 9 exposure. (4) For two-person mode, apply least-misery on aversive dimensions (Neuroticism-governed horror/darkness tolerance) and steer-to-middle on divergent Openness; treat aggregation as a tunable hypothesis to validate against actual joint-watch outcomes (the North Star metric). (5) HARD EXCLUDE every CA-specific element: no behavioural-trace inference, no covert profiling, no third-party data, no inference of special-category attributes, no resale. DEPENDENCY: most valuable when combined with direct theme/genre preference elicitation — personality is the prior, stated preferences are the signal; do not ship personality inference as a standalone driver. The CA case is the negative exemplar that justifies the consent-respecting, transparent, minimisation-first posture, which aligns directly with the repo's §2 invariants and ADR-0012.
key_sources: Kosinski, Stillwell & Graepel (2013), 'Private traits and attributes are predictable from digital records of human behavior', PNAS 110(15):5802-5805 — free: https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1218772110 . Youyou, Kosinski & Stillwell (2015), 'Computer-based personality judgments are more accurate than those made by humans', PNAS — free. Matz, Kosinski, Nave & Stillwell (2017), 'Psychological targeting as an effective approach to digital mass persuasion', PNAS 114(48):12714-12719 — free: https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1710966114 ; with critiques Sharp et al. (2018) and Eckles, Gordon & Johnson (2018) plus author replies (all PNAS letters, free). Stanford GSB, 'The Science Behind Cambridge Analytica: Does Psychological Profiling Work?' — free: https://www.gsb.stanford.edu/insights/science-behind-cambridge-analytica-does-psychological-profiling-work . NBC News, 'Cambridge Analytica's effectiveness called into question' (2018) — free: https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/politics-news/cambridge-analytica-s-effectiveness-called-question-despite-alleged-facebook-data-n858256 . Hinds & Joinson, 'Is the Use of Personality Based Psychometrics by Cambridge Analytica Psychological Science's Nuclear Bomb Moment?' — free (PMC): https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7879781/ . The Conversation, 'Cambridge Analytica's secret psychographic tool is a ghost from the past' — free. Rentfrow & Gosling (2003), 'The do re mi's of everyday life', J Pers Soc Psychol — foundational Big-Five/media-preference work, paywalled. ICO guidance on special-category data — free: https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/lawful-basis/a-guide-to-lawful-basis/special-category-data/ .
